primary_diagnosis.name: Severe traumatic brain injury with expanding acute intracranial haemorrhage and early uncal (transtentorial) herniation

pathophysiology_summary: Blunt occipital impact with a decelerating fall produced an intracranial haemorrhage (extradural, subdural or parenchymal) that is expanding within the fixed-volume cranial vault. As the haematoma grows it exhausts the compensatory CSF/venous reserve, intracranial pressure rises, and the medial temporal lobe (uncus) is forced across the tentorial edge, compressing the ipsilateral third cranial nerve (fixed, dilating right pupil = anisocoria) and the midbrain. Rising ICP triggers the Cushing reflex (hypertension with reflex bradycardia) and brainstem irritation drives the repetitive vomiting and declining GCS. Untreated, herniation progresses to brainstem compression, apnoea and death within a short, unforgiving window. Definitive management is emergency non-contrast CT head and neurosurgical decompression/haematoma evacuation; the prehospital priority is airway protection, ICP-aware positioning and the fastest possible transfer to a neurosurgical centre.

differential.evidence_basis: High-energy occipital impact with witnessed LOC, amnesia, repeated vomiting and progressive drowsiness — the classic evolving space-occupying bleed picture.
Same mechanism can cause mild TBI, but repeated vomiting, declining conscious level and anisocoria place this well beyond concussion — the dangerous mislabel to avoid.
Possible earlier drinking could confound conscious level, but cannot explain a unilateral fixed dilating pupil or the temporal link to a high-impact trauma.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the opening account the AI Doctor should already treat this as a possible expanding intracranial bleed and move toward emergency escalation, not a home-observation concussion pathway.

differential.diagnosis: expanding intracranial haemorrhage with raised ICP / early herniation
differential.evidence_basis: Anisocoria (fixed dilating right pupil) + declining GCS + repeated vomiting after occipital trauma = herniation until proven otherwise.
Classic lucid interval then rapid deterioration; a specific subtype needing the same immediate CT and neurosurgical pathway.
Common after occipital deceleration injury; indistinguishable from extradural without imaging but managed identically in the prehospital window.
Excluded as the working diagnosis by the objective red flags — anisocoria and falling GCS are structural, not concussive.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The presence of anisocoria plus a declining conscious level is the pivot: any pathway that keeps the patient at home is a critical failure at this stage.

differential.diagnosis: severe TBI with expanding intracranial haemorrhage and early uncal herniation
differential.evidence_basis: Diagnostic tetrad complete: altered/declining GCS, witnessed LOC with amnesia, anisocoria, and clinical signs of raised ICP (Cushing reflex on sealed vitals) — a neurosurgical emergency confirmed only by CT in hospital.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot confirm the bleed or its subtype; the correct end-state is immediate 000/Priority-1 transfer to a neurosurgical centre, not a remote diagnosis.

red_flags.description: Witnessed loss of consciousness (~2 minutes) after a high-energy head impact
red_flags.clinical_significance: LOC marks significant transfer of force to the brain and raises the probability of structural injury requiring imaging.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-appreciating injury severity and defaulting to a concussion/home-watch pathway.

red_flags.description: Post-traumatic amnesia and persistent confusion (repetitive questioning)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Ongoing amnesia/confusion indicates significant brain dysfunction and mandates urgent imaging under head-injury rules.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed indicator of moderate-to-severe TBI; delayed CT and neurosurgical referral.

red_flags.description: Repeated forceful vomiting (>2 episodes) after head injury
red_flags.clinical_significance: More than one episode of post-traumatic vomiting is a validated red flag for intracranial injury and, with reduced GCS, a major aspiration risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed raised-ICP sign and unguarded airway leading to aspiration in a drowsy patient.

red_flags.description: Progressive decline in conscious level (increasing drowsiness, harder to rouse)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A falling GCS after head injury signals an expanding lesion and impending herniation — the single most time-critical trajectory.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to escalate before brainstem compression and respiratory arrest.

red_flags.clinical_significance: A blown pupil after head trauma indicates third-nerve compression from uncal herniation — a hyperacute neurosurgical emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Herniation progresses to fatal brainstem compression within minutes to a short window.

red_flags.description: Cushing reflex — hypertension with relative bradycardia (clinician-measured, sealed vitals)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Hypertension plus bradycardia in a deteriorating head injury is a late physiological sign of dangerously raised intracranial pressure.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Misreading a low heart rate as 'stable' rather than as a preterminal ICP sign.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Framing a brief LOC plus vomiting as a simple concussion and advising home observation, when the anisocoria and declining GCS define a structural, expanding injury.
Blaming the drowsiness and confusion on possible alcohol and discounting the neurological signs — intoxication never explains a unilateral fixed pupil.
Interpreting bradycardia with hypertension as reassuring stable vitals rather than as a late, dangerous sign of raised intracranial pressure.
Not planning for the airway in a vomiting patient with a falling conscious level — aspiration is an immediate, preventable cause of harm while awaiting the ambulance.
Trying to assess GCS or 'watch and recheck' remotely instead of triggering an immediate emergency response — no remote observation is safe here.

risk_scores.clinical_implication: A GCS of 13 after trauma is already abnormal; a downward trend mandates immediate neurosurgical-centre transfer and airway readiness.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Multiple criteria met (GCS <15 at 2h, >1 vomiting episode, focal neurological deficit — anisocoria, signs of raised ICP)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Mandates an immediate non-contrast CT head within the emergency pathway — achievable only after hospital transfer.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Formal Glasgow Coma Scale and its trend
Objective blood pressure and heart rate (Cushing reflex)
Formal pupillary size, symmetry and light reaction
Presence and nature of any basilar skull fracture signs
Motor asymmetry / focal neurological deficits on examination
Non-contrast CT head — the definitive test for haemorrhage, midline shift and cerebral oedema
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 call requesting a Priority 1 / Intensive Care Paramedic response, explicitly stating a deteriorating head injury with an unequal pupil; keep the airway protected, do not move him upright, and if he vomits while drowsy log-roll him onto his side as a single unit with head/neck support.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The real risk is UNDER-action — mislabelling this as concussion or intoxication and advising home observation. The exact bleed subtype (extradural vs subdural vs parenchymal) cannot and need not be determined remotely; it does not change the immediate emergency action.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NICE NG232 Head injury: assessment and early management (2023)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: GCS <15, more than one episode of vomiting, and focal neurological deficit each mandate urgent CT head and emergency transfer; suspected raised ICP requires immediate specialist neurosurgical involvement.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Brain Trauma Foundation Guidelines for the Management of Severe TBI (4th ed.)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Avoid hypoxia and hypotension, protect the airway, and transfer without delay to a centre with neurosurgical capability; signs of herniation warrant immediate escalation.
